scenario: 💬 Idiom: 'Cost an arm and a leg'
text: The idiom '{{c1::It cost an arm and a leg}}' means something was extremely expensive.
explanation:
Pronunciation (stress-timed rhythm):
'it COS·tan·ARM·an·uh·LEG'
• Stress falls on COST, ARM, LEG — content words
• 'an' → /ən/ (schwa); 'and a' → /ən·ə/ both reduced
• The idiom always takes past tense: 'cost' (not 'costs')

🧠 Origin: Possibly from 18th century portrait painting — full-body portraits with arms and legs visible cost more.
usage:
• "That hotel in New York? It cost an arm and a leg."
  • "I need a new laptop but they're gonna cost an arm and a leg right now."
  • "Synonyms: 'a small fortune', 'a fortune', 'an absolute steal' (the opposite — very cheap)"
  • Context: casual conversation, never formal writing
spanish: 'Cost an arm and a leg' = costar un ojo de la cara / costar un riñón. Pronunciación: 'it COS-tan-ARM-an-uh-LEG' — el ritmo acompasado del inglés comprime 'an', 'and', 'a' en sonidos rápidos de schwa. Solo en pasado: 'cost' (no 'costs').

text: {{c1::"I'm on the fence"}} means you haven't decided yet — you're neutral or undecided about something.
explanation:
Pronunciation: 'I'm on the fence' → /aɪm·ɑn·ðuh·fɛns/
• 'on the' → /ɑn·ðuh/ — schwa in 'the'
• 'the fence' → linking: /ðuh·fɛns/ — 'the' and 'fence' link smoothly

🧠 Visual image: sitting on a fence between two sides, not committing to either. Perfect for non-committal social responses.

Related phrases:
• 'I could go either way.' — equally fine with both options
• 'I haven't made up my mind yet.'
• 'I'm torn.' — you want both, or feel conflicted
usage:
• "Should I take the job offer?" — "Honestly, I'm still on the fence."
  • "Are you coming tonight?" — "I'm on the fence — depends how tired I am."
  • "What do you think about [political topic]?" — "I'm kind of on the fence on that one."
spanish: 'I'm on the fence' = estoy indeciso / no me he decidido todavía. Pronunciación: /aɪm·ɑn·ðuh·fɛns/ — 'the' se reduce a schwa /ðuh/. Relacionado: 'I could go either way' (cualquier opción me parece bien), 'I'm torn' (estoy dividido).

text: {{c1::"That rings a bell"}} means something sounds familiar — you vaguely remember it.
explanation:
Pronunciation: 'that rings a bell' → /ðæ·trɪŋz·ə·bɛl/
• Resyllabification (C+V linking): 'that' + 'rings' → the /t/ of 'that' attaches to 'rings': /ðæt·rɪŋz/ — smooth, no pause
• 'a' → /ə/ (schwa: unstressed article)

🧠 Usage context: When someone mentions a name, place, or event and you half-remember it. Response to 'Do you know Maria from accounting?' — 'That rings a bell — does she have short hair?'
usage:
• "Her name rings a bell but I can't place her."
  • "That movie rings a bell — was it from the 90s?"
  • Stronger: 'That sounds very familiar.' / 'I think I know what you mean.'
  • Negative: 'That doesn't ring a bell at all.' / 'I'm drawing a blank.'
spanish: 'That rings a bell' = eso me suena / me resulta familiar. Pronunciación: /ðæ·trɪŋz·ə·bɛl/ — Resyllabification: la /t/ de 'that' se une a 'rings'. La 'a' se reduce a schwa. Opuesto: 'I'm drawing a blank' = no recuerdo nada.

text: {{c1::"Hit the ground running"}} means to start something immediately and with full energy — no ramp-up period.
explanation:
Pronunciation (stress-timed):
'HIT·ðuh·GROUND·RUN·nɪŋ'
• Stress: HIT, GROUND, RUN — content words
• 'the' → /ðuh/ (schwa)
• 'ground' → /ɡraʊnd/ — Dark L precedes? No L here, but unstressed middle gets compressed

🧠 Common in professional contexts: new job, new project, new semester. Often heard in hiring: 'We need someone who can hit the ground running from day one.'
usage:
• "She was new but really hit the ground running on the project."
  • "I want to hit the ground running when I start Monday."
  • Professional context: Interview answer: 'I've worked with this tech stack before, so I can hit the ground running.'
  • Synonym: 'jump right in', 'dive in head first'
spanish: 'Hit the ground running' = empezar de lleno / arrancar con todo desde el inicio. Muy común en contextos profesionales: contratación, proyectos nuevos. Ritmo: HIT-ðuh-GROUND-RUN-nɪŋ — el inglés acompasado por el estrés comprime 'the' a schwa.

text: {{c1::"Bite the bullet"}} means to endure a painful or difficult situation because you have no other choice.
explanation:
Pronunciation: 'bite the bullet' → /baɪt·ðuh·ˈbʊ·lɪt/
• 'the' → /ðuh/ (schwa)
• 'bullet' → /ˈbʊ·lɪt/ — Flap T: the /t/ in 'bullet' between /ʊ/ and /ɪ/ is a tap [ɾ]: /ˈbʊ·lɪt/ (some speakers use full /t/ here, but many use a soft tap in fast speech)

🧠 Origin: Soldiers before anesthesia would literally bite a bullet during surgery to endure pain.

Modern use: Any unpleasant-but-necessary action: expensive car repair, difficult conversation, boring but required task.
usage:
• "I hate going to the dentist but I just had to bite the bullet and make an appointment."
  • "Sometimes you just gotta bite the bullet and do the thing you've been putting off."
  • Synonyms: 'suck it up', 'take the hit', 'grin and bear it', 'just do it'
spanish: 'Bite the bullet' = tragarse el sapo / aguantar lo que toca. Para situaciones dolorosas pero necesarias. Pronunciación: /baɪt·ðuh·ˈbʊ·lɪt/ — 'the' → schwa, 'bullet' puede tener Flap T en habla rápida.

text: {{c1::"I'm swamped"}} and {{c1::"I'm slammed"}} both mean extremely busy — too much work and not enough time.
explanation:
Pronunciation:
• 'I'm swamped' → /aɪm·swɑːmpt/ — final consonant cluster: /-mpt/
• 'I'm slammed' → /aɪm·slæmd/

🧠 Register: Both very colloquial — fine in work Slack/email, not in formal writing.
• 'I'm swamped' = full like a swamp (overwhelmed by volume)
• 'I'm slammed' = hit hard by work load (more intense)
• 'Buried' = also common: 'I'm totally buried this week'
• Professional alternative: 'I have a packed schedule this week.'
usage:
• "Hey, want to grab lunch?" — "Can't today, I'm slammed."
  • "I'd love to help but I'm completely swamped with the launch."
  • "My inbox is swamped — I'll get back to you tomorrow."
  • Gradations: busy → hectic → swamped → slammed → underwater (worst)
spanish: 'I'm swamped' / 'I'm slammed' = estoy hasta el cuello / tengo demasiado trabajo. Muy coloquiales — perfectas para Slack o emails informales. Gradaciones: busy → hectic → swamped → slammed → underwater (la peor).

text: In American English, '{{c1::grab}}' replaces many verbs: grab a coffee (get), grab your coat (take), grab a seat (sit down), grab lunch (eat quickly).
explanation:
'Grab' is one of the most versatile colloquial verbs in American English. It implies speed, informality, and action.

Pronunciation: 'grab a' → /ɡræ·bə/ — linking: /b/ + /ə/ → smooth tap
• 'Let me grab that' → /lɛ·mi·ɡræb·ðæt/ (chunks + linking)
• 'Lemme' → /ˈlɛ·mi/ — lexical chunk (Let me → lemme)

🧠 In a café: 'Can I grab you a coffee?' = would you like me to get you a coffee? It's an offer, not a question about grabbing things.
usage:
• "Want to grab lunch later?" = let's eat lunch together
  • "Let me grab my keys." = I'll go get my keys
  • "Can you grab that for me?" = can you take/fetch that?
  • "Grab a seat wherever you like." = sit anywhere
  • "I'm gonna grab a quick shower." = take a quick shower
  • "Can I grab you something to drink?" = can I get you something?
spanish: 'Grab' en inglés americano reemplaza a 'get', 'take', 'eat', 'drink', 'sit' — un verbo multiusos informal. 'grab a coffee' = tomar un café, 'grab lunch' = ir a comer, 'grab a seat' = siéntate. Pronunciación: 'grab a' → /ɡræ·bə/ — enlace suave.

text: {{c1::"No worries"}} and {{c1::"No biggie"}} are casual ways to say 'it's fine' or 'don't apologize — it's not a problem.'
explanation:
Pronunciation:
• 'No worries' → /noʊ·ˈwɜː·riːz/ — Dark L? No L here, but note: 'worries' has the schwa-like /ɜː/ (stressed)
• 'No biggie' → /noʊ·ˈbɪ·ɡi/ — Flap T in 'biggie'? No /t/ here — but the /-gi/ ending is very soft

🧠 Context: Used when someone apologizes for something minor. Very Australian in origin but completely standard in the US now.

Register escalation:
• Very casual: 'No worries!' / 'No biggie!' / 'All good!'
• Neutral: 'It's fine.' / 'Not a problem.'
• Formal: 'Please don't worry about it.' / 'It's not an issue at all.'
usage:
• "Oh sorry, I'm late!" — "No worries! We just got here."
  • "I forgot to send that email." — "No biggie, I'll resend it."
  • "All good!" = common alternative (very American/Australian)
  • "Don't sweat it." = idiom: don't stress about it
spanish: 'No worries' / 'No biggie' = no hay problema / no te preocupes / está todo bien. Muy coloquial. Escala: No biggie! (muy casual) → It's fine. (neutral) → Please don't worry about it. (formal). 'Don't sweat it' = otro idioma: no te estreses por eso.
